title: Improving Coordination of Agriculture Research and Data Act
short_title: Improving Coordination of Agriculture Research and Data Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Agriculture to create two new groups to help farmers deal with climate change. The first group is a committee of eighteen experts who will study climate research for farming and give advice on what the government should focus on. The second group is a network that will share climate information, research, and help directly with farmers and rural communities. The Department of Agriculture must also write a report for Congress explaining how to help farmers become more prepared for climate change.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Conservation, Research, and Biotechnology.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Coordination of Agriculture Research and Data Act.

sections.title: Section 2. Purposes
sections.text: This law has three main goals. First, it wants to better organize how we study climate and farming together and how we collect information about it. Second, it wants to make it easier to share helpful technology and knowledge with farmers. Third, it wants to create a national plan for studying how farming can deal with climate change by both reducing harmful effects and adapting to changes that are already happening.

sections.title: Section 3. Agriculture Climate Research Coordination and Data
sections.text: This section adds new parts to an existing law from nineteen seventy seven about agricultural research and teaching. It creates two new programs to help coordinate climate research in farming. The first program is called the Agriculture Climate Scientific Research Advisory Committee. This committee will be part of the Office of the Chief Scientist at the Department of Agriculture. The committee has eighteen members including the Secretary of Agriculture, the Chief Scientist, and other government officials, plus some members from outside the government. The committee will review and make suggestions about long term and short term national policies for collecting climate data related to farming. They will focus on areas like removing carbon from the air, keeping carbon in soil, reducing greenhouse gases from farming, keeping soil healthy, reducing methane gas from livestock, managing wetlands, and using smart farming practices that help the climate. The committee will identify gaps in current research and create a plan every two years for what research is most needed. Every five years, they will evaluate how well climate farming research is working and make recommendations. They will also review how the Department of Agriculture measures and evaluates new technology. The committee will work with industry groups, farmers, advocacy organizations, scientific groups, and colleges to get their input. They will coordinate with other federal agencies to avoid duplicating work and find opportunities to work together. The committee will review new climate technology and practices and make recommendations to the second new program. They will also suggest budget recommendations each year to support climate research. Committee members who are not already government employees will serve terms of up to six years, with some initial members serving shorter terms. The committee will elect a chairperson, vice chairperson, and seven member executive subcommittee each year. The Secretary can hire an executive director and up to three professional staff members to help the committee. Most committee members will not be paid, but they can be reimbursed for travel expenses. The second program is called the Rural Climate Alliance Network. This network will be run through the Climate Hubs and will coordinate providing climate research, data, extension services, and technical help to farmers, the food industry, nonprofit organizations, and researchers. The network will improve responses to climate disasters, communication about climate risks, education and technology transfer in appropriate languages, tools to prevent damage from extreme weather and pest outbreaks, communication between farmers and researchers, coordination with other federal agencies, and coordination with private companies in the food supply chain. The network will include many types of organizations such as colleges and universities, state cooperative institutions, state extension services, state agriculture departments, state agricultural experiment stations, Indian tribes, qualified nonprofit organizations and private businesses, farmer groups, agencies within the Department of Agriculture, and other qualified technical assistance providers. It will also include existing climate research networks within the Department of Agriculture and other government affiliated entities outside the department. Network members can start, expand, or continue agricultural research programs that build capacity to deal with climate change disasters. They can provide information about agricultural climate issues, respond to climate impacts, collect and share agricultural climate data, help farmers implement new management approaches, transfer technology and information, and improve climate change awareness and education. They can do this through developing phone apps, websites, handbooks, warning systems, and other communication tools in appropriate languages. They can provide training programs for various agricultural professionals and develop curriculum for managing climate risks. Within one year of this law being passed, the Secretary must submit a report to Congress describing current needs for climate research and data systems in food and agriculture, progress made toward developing climate solutions, technical assistance needs of rural communities and farmers, actions and recommendations of the advisory committee, and recommended federal budget activities and funding levels. The report must include an inventory of current efforts to support climate adaptation and mitigation tools for farmers by various government levels and organizations, a baseline report on soil carbon sequestration, a description of challenges faced by farmers that impact their climate resilience, a long term strategy for responding to those challenges, and an evaluation of how climate change challenges impact the agricultural workforce, production, rural families and communities, and succession planning.
